text: The whole world's a jungle, huh, Mayo? Dog eat dog down to the last one, right?
en: The whole world's a jungle, huh, Mayo? Dog eat dog down to the last one, right?

text: You got it, Sweet Pea.  Nice boonies, Seeger.
en: You got it, Sweet Pea.  Nice boonies, Seeger.

text: Who's got two bucks? It's costing me every penny they pay us just to keep my old Lady and my kids in that motel.
en: Who's got two bucks? It's costing me every penny they pay us just to keep my old Lady and my kids in that motel.

text: I don't notice anyone else complaining.
en: I don't notice anyone else complaining.

text: Not if you make it worth my while. How about free boonies for the duration?
en: Not if you make it worth my while. How about free boonies for the duration?

text: It's grown out more than an inch, sweetheart.
en: It's grown out more than an inch, sweetheart.

text: Come on. Let's go dance.
en: Come on. Let's go dance.

text: Yes, ma'am. Four long, hard weeks of sacrifice for my country... for my people... for you. But I survived.
en: Yes, ma'am. Four long, hard weeks of sacrifice for my country... for my people... for you. But I survived.

text: Polish. What kind of name is Mayo?
en: Polish. What kind of name is Mayo?

text: Italian. My mom was Irish. I got her ears. But the rest is all wop.
en: Italian. My mom was Irish. I got her ears. But the rest is all wop.

text: Where are you from, Mayo the Wop?
en: Where are you from, Mayo the Wop?

text: Everywhere and nowhere, Paula the Polack.
en: Everywhere and nowhere, Paula the Polack.

text: Seriously.
en: Seriously.

text: My father is a Rear Admiral in the Seventh Fleet.
en: My father is a Rear Admiral in the Seventh Fleet.

text: Really?
en: Really?

text: Yeah. We've lived all over the world. Katmandu, Moscow, Nairobi.
en: Yeah. We've lived all over the world. Katmandu, Moscow, Nairobi.

text: Really? I've never been out of Washington except once when I visited this aunt of mine over to Portland. I mean, over at Portland. Ain't it pathetic the way folks talk around here?
en: Really? I've never been out of Washington except once when I visited this aunt of mine over to Portland. I mean, over at Portland. Ain't it pathetic the way folks talk around here?

text: No, and I'm not looking for one either.
en: No, and I'm not looking for one either.

text: Not me.
en: Not me.

text: Yeah? Why're you here?
en: Yeah? Why're you here?

text: To meet interesting people, improve myself. You wouldn't believe the losers we got over in Port Angeles.
en: To meet interesting people, improve myself. You wouldn't believe the losers we got over in Port Angeles.

text: Do you go to school?
en: Do you go to school?

text: No. I work for National Paper. It's a good job. I make eight-twenty-three an hour.  When I get enough money saved, I plan to go on to college.
en: No. I work for National Paper. It's a good job. I make eight-twenty-three an hour.  When I get enough money saved, I plan to go on to college.

text: Cake walk. Both my dad and my brother went through it and made it, so I know I can.
en: Cake walk. Both my dad and my brother went through it and made it, so I know I can.

text: Is your brother a flyer?
en: Is your brother a flyer?

text: He was. He died.
en: He was. He died.

text: Vietnam?
en: Vietnam?

text: Yeah.
en: Yeah.

text: I had a big brother who died over there, too. He wasn't no flyer though. He was just your basic Marine Corps type. I was only twelve when it happened, so I don't remember much about him.
en: I had a big brother who died over there, too. He wasn't no flyer though. He was just your basic Marine Corps type. I was only twelve when it happened, so I don't remember much about him.

text: I sure remember Tommy.  Mind if we talked about something else?
en: I sure remember Tommy.  Mind if we talked about something else?

text: We don't have to talk at all.
en: We don't have to talk at all.

text: Who knows? Guys a lot smarter than me are dropping out like flies.
en: Who knows? Guys a lot smarter than me are dropping out like flies.

text: Just think 'I'm gonna do it!' Program yourself. See yourself making it. It'll happen. I know 'cause I just read this article in Cosmo, and it was about that very thing.
en: Just think 'I'm gonna do it!' Program yourself. See yourself making it. It'll happen. I know 'cause I just read this article in Cosmo, and it was about that very thing.

text: You're a very pretty girl, Paula.
en: You're a very pretty girl, Paula.

text: Now what on earth would give you an idea like that?
en: Now what on earth would give you an idea like that?

text: Don't worry. I'll respect you afterwards.
en: Don't worry. I'll respect you afterwards.

text: 'Nellie's Nymphos!'
en: 'Nellie's Nymphos!'

text: Jesus, that Lynette! I rode her hard and put her up wet.
en: Jesus, that Lynette! I rode her hard and put her up wet.

text: I got my foot on the floor.
en: I got my foot on the floor.

text: Great.
en: Great.

text: Details, Pokrif. From what I saw he had an incredible body.
en: Details, Pokrif. From what I saw he had an incredible body.

text: Yeah... Mmmm...
en: Yeah... Mmmm...

text: What did he do? Did he do anything that was different?
en: What did he do? Did he do anything that was different?

text: Everything was different.
en: Everything was different.

text: But in what ways?
en: But in what ways?

text: Big Sid came in about two and a half seconds, then had the nerve to ask, 'Did you make it, too, sweetheart?'
en: Big Sid came in about two and a half seconds, then had the nerve to ask, 'Did you make it, too, sweetheart?'

text: No, but I told him I'd be at the Town Tavern next Saturday night, and he sounded like he might come.
en: No, but I told him I'd be at the Town Tavern next Saturday night, and he sounded like he might come.

text: I told Zack about Saturday night, too. The fifth week's supposed to be the roughest. Come Wednesday, he'll be wishing he took my number.
en: I told Zack about Saturday night, too. The fifth week's supposed to be the roughest. Come Wednesday, he'll be wishing he took my number.

text: You hope.
en: You hope.

text: He'll show. I'd bet my paycheck on it.
en: He'll show. I'd bet my paycheck on it.

text: I said, come here!
en: I said, come here!

text: What are you tired from?
en: What are you tired from?

text: Come over here where I can see you.
en: Come over here where I can see you.

text: But you knew right off what I was talking about, didn't you, Paula! Did you let that boy --
en: But you knew right off what I was talking about, didn't you, Paula! Did you let that boy --

text: Well, as long as you live in this house, young lady, you live by my rules! You should be dating local boys.
en: Well, as long as you live in this house, young lady, you live by my rules! You should be dating local boys.

text: Uh-uh! Not a chance! There's nobody in this town doing anything with his life, except what his father did, which is nothing. If I can't have more out of life than that, I'd rather be dead!
en: Uh-uh! Not a chance! There's nobody in this town doing anything with his life, except what his father did, which is nothing. If I can't have more out of life than that, I'd rather be dead!

text: Do you honestly think you'll find a boy in that... that officer's school who's serious about marriage?
en: Do you honestly think you'll find a boy in that... that officer's school who's serious about marriage?

text: Yes I do!
en: Yes I do!

text: Then you're dumber than I thought! All you'll get from their kind is pregnant!
en: Then you're dumber than I thought! All you'll get from their kind is pregnant!

text: Yes, Joe.
en: Yes, Joe.

text: When did this happen?
en: When did this happen?

text: A long time ago.
en: A long time ago.

text: Shhhh...
en: Shhhh...

text: She'll come, pard. A rich socialite Oakie like you oughta be a big catch around these parts.
en: She'll come, pard. A rich socialite Oakie like you oughta be a big catch around these parts.

text: Get off my case, Mayo. I didn't grow up rich.
en: Get off my case, Mayo. I didn't grow up rich.

text: Who cares?  Mmmm. Now I remember. Mayo the Wop. Gee, I'm glad you're here. I've been looking forward to this all week.
en: Who cares?  Mmmm. Now I remember. Mayo the Wop. Gee, I'm glad you're here. I've been looking forward to this all week.

text: Me, too.
en: Me, too.

text: Like pick up some booze and go to a motel?
en: Like pick up some booze and go to a motel?

text: Or we could do that yeah.
en: Or we could do that yeah.

text: My kinda group!
en: My kinda group!

text: Lynette, just keep your mouth shut until we get to the motel. Will you do that for me, please.
en: Lynette, just keep your mouth shut until we get to the motel. Will you do that for me, please.

text: Well, excuse me for livin'!
en: Well, excuse me for livin'!

text: He didn't give you much choice.
en: He didn't give you much choice.

text: There's always a choice.
en: There's always a choice.

text: Where'd you learn to fight like that?
en: Where'd you learn to fight like that?

text: I don't feel like talking, if you don't mind.
en: I don't feel like talking, if you don't mind.

text: Opening up just a little wouldn't kill you, ya know.
en: Opening up just a little wouldn't kill you, ya know.

text: Where's that coming from? I wouldn't fuck now if my life depended on it!
en: Where's that coming from? I wouldn't fuck now if my life depended on it!

text: Forget it. Just get out of here.
en: Forget it. Just get out of here.

text: Be a friend. Leave.
en: Be a friend. Leave.

text: You got no manners and you never tell the truth! You're nothin' special. And if you ask me, you got no chance at all of being an officer!
en: You got no manners and you never tell the truth! You're nothin' special. And if you ask me, you got no chance at all of being an officer!

text: Wrong. I've driven a hundred and twenty miles, told a hundred and twenty lies, and said a hundred and twenty Hail Mary's since I saw you. Hungry?
en: Wrong. I've driven a hundred and twenty miles, told a hundred and twenty lies, and said a hundred and twenty Hail Mary's since I saw you. Hungry?

text: I'm starving.
en: I'm starving.

text: I know who you are and what you want.
en: I know who you are and what you want.

text: What do you want, Paula? What do you really want?
en: What do you want, Paula? What do you really want?

text: To have a good time with you until you have to go.
en: To have a good time with you until you have to go.

text: That's it?
en: That's it?

text: You're better than candy.
en: You're better than candy.

text: I'm serious. It's gonna be hard to get enough.
en: I'm serious. It's gonna be hard to get enough.

text: I've never had a girl.
en: I've never had a girl.

text: Any time, sailor.
en: Any time, sailor.

text: Sure.
en: Sure.

text: I can't risk it.
en: I can't risk it.

text: You'd make it. He's just getting to the girls. Come on, Zack. I gotta see my family, man. I couldn't take it if he keeps me here over the weekend.
en: You'd make it. He's just getting to the girls. Come on, Zack. I gotta see my family, man. I couldn't take it if he keeps me here over the weekend.

text: Sorry, pard. Wouldn't want you to get an honor violation.
en: Sorry, pard. Wouldn't want you to get an honor violation.

text: No, sir. You can kick me out, but I'm not quitting.
en: No, sir. You can kick me out, but I'm not quitting.

text: Get into your fatigues, Mayo. Before the weekend's out, you'll quit.
en: Get into your fatigues, Mayo. Before the weekend's out, you'll quit.

text: No, sir! It was the hardest thing I ever did, sir! Until this.
en: No, sir! It was the hardest thing I ever did, sir! Until this.

text: That's a lie, Mayo. You've gone through a lot worse, haven't you?
en: That's a lie, Mayo. You've gone through a lot worse, haven't you?

text: I'm doing okay, sir.
en: I'm doing okay, sir.

text: No you're not. You're failing the big one, baby, and I don't just mean in here. I mean in life. I've watched you, Mayo, and you don't mesh. You grab-ass and joke around but you don't make friends, not the way the others do.
en: No you're not. You're failing the big one, baby, and I don't just mean in here. I mean in life. I've watched you, Mayo, and you don't mesh. You grab-ass and joke around but you don't make friends, not the way the others do.

text: Sir, this candidate believes he'll make a good officer, sir!
en: Sir, this candidate believes he'll make a good officer, sir!

text: No way, Mayo. You don't give a shit about anybody but yourself and every single one of your classmates knows it. Think they'd trust you behind the controls of a plane they have to fly in? Hey, man, I figure you for the kind of guy who'd zip off one day in my F-14 and sell it to the Cubans.
en: No way, Mayo. You don't give a shit about anybody but yourself and every single one of your classmates knows it. Think they'd trust you behind the controls of a plane they have to fly in? Hey, man, I figure you for the kind of guy who'd zip off one day in my F-14 and sell it to the Cubans.

text: Sir, that's not true! I love my country!
en: Sir, that's not true! I love my country!

text: Sell it to the Air Force, Mayo!
en: Sell it to the Air Force, Mayo!

text: That's no reason. Everybody wants to fly. My grandmother wants to fly. You going after a job with one of the airlines?
en: That's no reason. Everybody wants to fly. My grandmother wants to fly. You going after a job with one of the airlines?

text: I want to fly jets, sir!
en: I want to fly jets, sir!

text: Why? Because you can do it alone?
en: Why? Because you can do it alone?

text: No, sir!
en: No, sir!

text: What is it, the kicks? Is that it?
en: What is it, the kicks? Is that it?

text: I don't want to do something anybody can do.
en: I don't want to do something anybody can do.

text: Pity you don't have the character.
en: Pity you don't have the character.

text: That's not true, sir! I've changed a lot since I've been here! And I'm gonna make it, sir!
en: That's not true, sir! I've changed a lot since I've been here! And I'm gonna make it, sir!

text: Not a fucking chance, asshole!
en: Not a fucking chance, asshole!

text: Yes, sir!
en: Yes, sir!

text: Maybe there's hope for you yet.
en: Maybe there's hope for you yet.

text: Hey, Sid, thanks.
en: Hey, Sid, thanks.

text: Yeah.
en: Yeah.

text: Yeah. All week long.
en: Yeah. All week long.

text: Naw.
en: Naw.

text: A little.
en: A little.

text: He said he grew up poor?
en: He said he grew up poor?

text: The kid on the windy side of the baker's window. That's how he put it.
en: The kid on the windy side of the baker's window. That's how he put it.

text: Foley's not poor. Buddy of mine in oh-four told me he's the son of a rich doctor down in Louisiana.
en: Foley's not poor. Buddy of mine in oh-four told me he's the son of a rich doctor down in Louisiana.

text: It sure was.
en: It sure was.

text: I'll get it if you want.
en: I'll get it if you want.

text: I don't want you to move.
en: I don't want you to move.

text: I don't want to move. But somebody has to move sometime. Eventually.
en: I don't want to move. But somebody has to move sometime. Eventually.

text: They found them like that, shriveled up from weeks without food or water...
en: They found them like that, shriveled up from weeks without food or water...

text: What's stopping you?
en: What's stopping you?

text: That's no excuse for not going after what you want.
en: That's no excuse for not going after what you want.

text: Who says I'm not going after what I want? My mother's thirty-nine years old and she still works in that factory. Every time I see her, I know exactly what I don't want.
en: Who says I'm not going after what I want? My mother's thirty-nine years old and she still works in that factory. Every time I see her, I know exactly what I don't want.

text: God.
en: God.

text: The thing that really got to me... she didn't leave a note. Nothing. I've always hated her for that.
en: The thing that really got to me... she didn't leave a note. Nothing. I've always hated her for that.

text: Does it still hurt?
en: Does it still hurt?

text: Naw. You're alone in this world no matter what kinda folks or background you had. Nothing hurts, pard, once you got that one down.
en: Naw. You're alone in this world no matter what kinda folks or background you had. Nothing hurts, pard, once you got that one down.

text: I understand. Maybe we'll see each other after the show...
en: I understand. Maybe we'll see each other after the show...

text: Nobody. Just a girl I've been making it with the last couple of weekends.
en: Nobody. Just a girl I've been making it with the last couple of weekends.

text: Great ass.
en: Great ass.

text: Yeah, I sort of thought so myself.
en: Yeah, I sort of thought so myself.

text: Better watch out for that kind, Zackie. You know what they call 'em, don't ya?
en: Better watch out for that kind, Zackie. You know what they call 'em, don't ya?

text: Yeah, I know.
en: Yeah, I know.

text: Back east in Newport, Rhode Island, they call 'em the Fall River Debs. In Pensacola, the Mobile Debs. In Norfolk --
en: Back east in Newport, Rhode Island, they call 'em the Fall River Debs. In Pensacola, the Mobile Debs. In Norfolk --

text: That what she was... a Norfolk Deb?
en: That what she was... a Norfolk Deb?

text: Who?  Aw shit, Zackie, let's not get off on your mother again, please.
en: Who?  Aw shit, Zackie, let's not get off on your mother again, please.

text: What if I want to talk about her, pard? What then? You know, that's all I've ever heard from you, since I was a kid... you never want to talk about that, man, and it's important.
en: What if I want to talk about her, pard? What then? You know, that's all I've ever heard from you, since I was a kid... you never want to talk about that, man, and it's important.

text: There's nothing to talk about. Two goddamn times I made it with your old lady. We barely even talked.
en: There's nothing to talk about. Two goddamn times I made it with your old lady. We barely even talked.

text: I wrote. Not every week...
en: I wrote. Not every week...

text: She said you told her in every letter how much you loved her, how you wanted to marry her, have children with her...
en: She said you told her in every letter how much you loved her, how you wanted to marry her, have children with her...

text: I never said any of that!
en: I never said any of that!

text: I found them, pard, and read them myself, right after she did it!
en: I found them, pard, and read them myself, right after she did it!

text: Okay, I wrote those things... and yeah, I had big thoughts of getting together with your mom... but when she hit me with being pregnant, I saw who she was. I'd had quiff lay that shit on me before!
en: Okay, I wrote those things... and yeah, I had big thoughts of getting together with your mom... but when she hit me with being pregnant, I saw who she was. I'd had quiff lay that shit on me before!

text: What did you call her? What did I hear you call her, you son of a bitch?
en: What did you call her? What did I hear you call her, you son of a bitch?

text: Nothing. Go back to the show, Paula.
en: Nothing. Go back to the show, Paula.

text: I've seen all that a hundred times.
en: I've seen all that a hundred times.

text: Hey, will you just leave me alone?
en: Hey, will you just leave me alone?

text: Zack, I don't know if I want to do that...
en: Zack, I don't know if I want to do that...

text: When I'm good and ready.
en: When I'm good and ready.

text: I haven't made up my mind.
en: I haven't made up my mind.

text: What do you mean?
en: What do you mean?

text: You know what I mean. Would you... let yourself get pregnant?
en: You know what I mean. Would you... let yourself get pregnant?

text: No way... Would you?
en: No way... Would you?

text: I never used to think I'd do something like that, but now I'm not so sure. You ask me, nine weeks just ain't long enough to get a guy to fall in love with you.
en: I never used to think I'd do something like that, but now I'm not so sure. You ask me, nine weeks just ain't long enough to get a guy to fall in love with you.

text: That don't justify trying to trap a boy by getting pregnant, Lynette! Nothing justifies that. I can't believe you're even thinking like that. I mean, that's really backward.
en: That don't justify trying to trap a boy by getting pregnant, Lynette! Nothing justifies that. I can't believe you're even thinking like that. I mean, that's really backward.

text: No more backward, if you ask me, than the way these hotshot assholes fuck us, then ditch us.  Don't you ever feel used, Paula? Don't you ever feel like if this is all you get for your trouble then the sonofabitch ought to be paying for it...?
en: No more backward, if you ask me, than the way these hotshot assholes fuck us, then ditch us.  Don't you ever feel used, Paula? Don't you ever feel like if this is all you get for your trouble then the sonofabitch ought to be paying for it...?

text: No. I never feel like that.
en: No. I never feel like that.

text: I do.
en: I do.

text: Are those for me?
en: Are those for me?

text: No, they're for your mom.
en: No, they're for your mom.

text: I don't mean anything by it.
en: I don't mean anything by it.

text: No, it's okay. It was a great free meal. Everybody was so uptight I felt sorry for you.
en: No, it's okay. It was a great free meal. Everybody was so uptight I felt sorry for you.

text: That's okay. I'm used to it.
en: That's okay. I'm used to it.

text: Yeah, then if I get jets, it's on to Beeville, Texas.
en: Yeah, then if I get jets, it's on to Beeville, Texas.

text: No. Is that what you want?
en: No. Is that what you want?

text: Some day. When I'm sure I can do a better job of it than my folks.
en: Some day. When I'm sure I can do a better job of it than my folks.

text: What would you do differently?
en: What would you do differently?

text: For a start, I wouldn't marry a man I wasn't in love with.
en: For a start, I wouldn't marry a man I wasn't in love with.

text: Why'd your mom marry that guy if she didn't love him?
en: Why'd your mom marry that guy if she didn't love him?

text: Because my real father wouldn't marry her.
en: Because my real father wouldn't marry her.

text: Your real father?
en: Your real father?

text: Twenty-two years ago.
en: Twenty-two years ago.

text: No wonder he was looking at me like that.
en: No wonder he was looking at me like that.

text: I'll try, but this week we go into survival training, so I can't make any promises.  Well, thanks again for dinner. Thank your mom again for me, will you?
en: I'll try, but this week we go into survival training, so I can't make any promises.  Well, thanks again for dinner. Thank your mom again for me, will you?

text: Sure.  Zack, I hope you know I didn't have to show you that picture.
en: Sure.  Zack, I hope you know I didn't have to show you that picture.

text: I know that.
en: I know that.

text: You'd better get smart, man. It's time to walk away.
en: You'd better get smart, man. It's time to walk away.

text: What? You've gotta be kidding!
en: What? You've gotta be kidding!

text: Remember what Foley said? His little warning? Those are the girls he was talking about. They're out to marry us any way they can.
en: Remember what Foley said? His little warning? Those are the girls he was talking about. They're out to marry us any way they can.

text: I don't believe that. They're just having a good time, same as us.
en: I don't believe that. They're just having a good time, same as us.

text: That's what they want you to think, but I saw where she lived, what is she's trying to get away from.  Just take my word for it, pard. Break it off now. Do it this week.
en: That's what they want you to think, but I saw where she lived, what is she's trying to get away from.  Just take my word for it, pard. Break it off now. Do it this week.

text: Ashamed? No -- I love you, Lynette. I mean that. After I leave them, I'll meet you at the motel, okay?
en: Ashamed? No -- I love you, Lynette. I mean that. After I leave them, I'll meet you at the motel, okay?

text: If you won't take me to dinner with your parents, I won't meet you at the motel.
en: If you won't take me to dinner with your parents, I won't meet you at the motel.

text: Lynette, I told you already, it won't work.
en: Lynette, I told you already, it won't work.

text: Then, I'll see you around.
en: Then, I'll see you around.

text: No problem, but who's Susan?
en: No problem, but who's Susan?

text: My girl back home. We're supposed to get married after I get my wings. She was Tommy's girl. They were engaged to be married before he died.  I should've told you about her. I don't know why I didn't, except I didn't want you to think I was a shit for making it with Lynette.
en: My girl back home. We're supposed to get married after I get my wings. She was Tommy's girl. They were engaged to be married before he died.  I should've told you about her. I don't know why I didn't, except I didn't want you to think I was a shit for making it with Lynette.

text: I'm not your folks, man. You love this... Susan?
en: I'm not your folks, man. You love this... Susan?

text: She's the sweetest person I've ever known. Loves kids. Works with handicapped kids every afternoon at the church. Everybody loves her.
en: She's the sweetest person I've ever known. Loves kids. Works with handicapped kids every afternoon at the church. Everybody loves her.

text: I didn't ask you all that, Sweet Pea. I asked if you loved her.
en: I didn't ask you all that, Sweet Pea. I asked if you loved her.

text: Listen, I'm not going to go to that little reunion party. I'm meeting Lynette at the motel.  Best head in fifty-two states. After three days of survival training, how could I resist?
en: Listen, I'm not going to go to that little reunion party. I'm meeting Lynette at the motel.  Best head in fifty-two states. After three days of survival training, how could I resist?

text: Lynette told me it really tore her up when you didn't call this week.
en: Lynette told me it really tore her up when you didn't call this week.

text: Just trying to have fun. That fucking prison is really starting to get to me. C'mon, Seeger. Gimme a push.  Fuck you guys! I'll do it myself!
en: Just trying to have fun. That fucking prison is really starting to get to me. C'mon, Seeger. Gimme a push.  Fuck you guys! I'll do it myself!

text: I'm a little late, that's all.
en: I'm a little late, that's all.

text: How late?
en: How late?

text: What difference does it make? If anything was to happen, which I'm sure it isn't, it would be my responsibility.
en: What difference does it make? If anything was to happen, which I'm sure it isn't, it would be my responsibility.

text: Exactly how late are you, Lynette?
en: Exactly how late are you, Lynette?

en: Maybe he'll have some advice.  Maybe he'll even talk to your father.

text: Are you kidding?  Don't be ridiculous.
en: Are you kidding?  Don't be ridiculous.

text: It's better than doing nothing.
en: It's better than doing nothing.

text: I'm sorry.  I, we
en: I'm sorry.  I, we

text: Okay.
en: Okay.

text: Actually, I'd like to talk to you alone.
en: Actually, I'd like to talk to you alone.

text: I couldn't stop myself.
en: I couldn't stop myself.

text: Maybe they don't want worldly things distracting me from my teaching.
en: Maybe they don't want worldly things distracting me from my teaching.

text: Why do you do it?  I mean, with all this seize-the-day business, I'd have thought you'd be out seeing the world or something?
en: Why do you do it?  I mean, with all this seize-the-day business, I'd have thought you'd be out seeing the world or something?

text: Ah, but I am seeing the world, Neil. The new world.  Seeing a student like you take root and bloom.  It's worth everything.  That's why I came back here. A place like this needs at least one teacher like me.  Did you come here to talk about my teaching?
en: Ah, but I am seeing the world, Neil. The new world.  Seeing a student like you take root and bloom.  It's worth everything.  That's why I came back here. A place like this needs at least one teacher like me.  Did you come here to talk about my teaching?

text: Mr. Keating, my father is making me quit the play at Henley Hall.  When I think about carpe diem and all that, I feel like I'm in prison!  I mean, I can see his point.  We're not a rich family like Charlie's.  But he's planned the rest of my life for me and he's never even asked me what I want!
en: Mr. Keating, my father is making me quit the play at Henley Hall.  When I think about carpe diem and all that, I feel like I'm in prison!  I mean, I can see his point.  We're not a rich family like Charlie's.  But he's planned the rest of my life for me and he's never even asked me what I want!

text: You can't live a life for someone else, Neil.  You can only live for yourself. Have you told your father what you just told me?  Have you shown him your passion about acting?
en: You can't live a life for someone else, Neil.  You can only live for yourself. Have you told your father what you just told me?  Have you shown him your passion about acting?

text: Are you kidding?  He'd kill me!
en: Are you kidding?  He'd kill me!

text: Then you're playing a part for him too, aren't you?  A dangerously self- destructive one.
en: Then you're playing a part for him too, aren't you?  A dangerously self- destructive one.

text: I know what he'll say.  He'll say that acting is just a whim and that it's frivolous and that I should forget about it.  He'll tell me how they're counting on me and to put it out of my mind "for my own good."
en: I know what he'll say.  He'll say that acting is just a whim and that it's frivolous and that I should forget about it.  He'll tell me how they're counting on me and to put it out of my mind "for my own good."

text: Well, if it's more than a whim, then you'll have to prove that to him.  You'll have to show him with your passion and commitment that it's what you really want to do.  If that doesn't work, at least by then you'll be eighteen and able to do what you want.
en: Well, if it's more than a whim, then you'll have to prove that to him.  You'll have to show him with your passion and commitment that it's what you really want to do.  If that doesn't work, at least by then you'll be eighteen and able to do what you want.

text: Eighteen!  That's two years!  What about the play?  The performance is tomorrow night!
en: Eighteen!  That's two years!  What about the play?  The performance is tomorrow night!

text: Give your father the benefit of the doubt.  Talk to him.  Let him see who you are.
en: Give your father the benefit of the doubt.  Talk to him.  Let him see who you are.

text: Isn't there an easier way?
en: Isn't there an easier way?

text: Not if you're going to stay true to yourself.
en: Not if you're going to stay true to yourself.

text: Why don't you read it to her? It worked for Nuwanda.
en: Why don't you read it to her? It worked for Nuwanda.

text: She won't even see me, Pitts.
en: She won't even see me, Pitts.

text: Nuwanda recited poetry to Gloria and she jumped all over him... right, Nuwanda?
en: Nuwanda recited poetry to Gloria and she jumped all over him... right, Nuwanda?

text: Knox!  what are you doing here?
en: Knox!  what are you doing here?

text: I don't care.  I love you, Chris.  You deserve better than Chet and I'm it. Please accept these.
en: I don't care.  I love you, Chris.  You deserve better than Chet and I'm it. Please accept these.

text: Knox, you're crazy.
en: Knox, you're crazy.

text: All I'm asking you to do is listen.  "The heavens made a girl named Chris, With hair and skin of gold To touch her would be paradise To kiss her glory untold."
en: All I'm asking you to do is listen.  "The heavens made a girl named Chris, With hair and skin of gold To touch her would be paradise To kiss her glory untold."

text: Yep.
en: Yep.

text: I don't know.
en: I don't know.

text: I'll tell you later.
en: I'll tell you later.

text: Yeah.
en: Yeah.

text: Really?  You told your father what you told me?  You let him see your passion for acting?
en: Really?  You told your father what you told me?  You let him see your passion for acting?

text: Yeah.  He didn't like it one bit but at least he's letting me stay in the play. Of course, he won't be able to come. He'll be in Chicago on business.  But I think he's gonna let me stay with acting.  As long as I keep my grades up.
en: Yeah.  He didn't like it one bit but at least he's letting me stay in the play. Of course, he won't be able to come. He'll be in Chicago on business.  But I think he's gonna let me stay with acting.  As long as I keep my grades up.

text: So much the better.
en: So much the better.

text: Knox, why are you doing this to me?
en: Knox, why are you doing this to me?

text: You can't be in here.
en: You can't be in here.

text: Oh, but it's fine for you to come barging into my school and make a complete fool out of me?
en: Oh, but it's fine for you to come barging into my school and make a complete fool out of me?

text: I didn't mean to make a fool of you.
en: I didn't mean to make a fool of you.

text: Well, you did!  Chet found out and he's nuts.  It took everything I could do to keep him from coming here and killing you. You have to stop this stuff, Knox.
en: Well, you did!  Chet found out and he's nuts.  It took everything I could do to keep him from coming here and killing you. You have to stop this stuff, Knox.

text: But I love you.
en: But I love you.

text: You say that over and over but you don't even know me!
en: You say that over and over but you don't even know me!

text: Just like that?!  You just knew?
en: Just like that?!  You just knew?

text: Of course just like that.  That's how you always know when it's right.
en: Of course just like that.  That's how you always know when it's right.

text: And if it so happens that you're wrong? If it just so happens that I could care less About you?
en: And if it so happens that you're wrong? If it just so happens that I could care less About you?

text: Then you wouldn't be here warning me about Chet.
en: Then you wouldn't be here warning me about Chet.

text: Are you going with Chet?
en: Are you going with Chet?

text: Chet?  To a play? Are you kidding?
en: Chet?  To a play? Are you kidding?

text: Then come with me.
en: Then come with me.

text: Knox, you are so infuriating!
en: Knox, you are so infuriating!

text: Just give me one chance.  If you don't like me after tonight, I'll stay away forever.
en: Just give me one chance.  If you don't like me after tonight, I'll stay away forever.

text: Uh-huh.
en: Uh-huh.

text: I promise.  Dead Poets honor.  Come with me tonight, then if you don't want to see me again, I swear I'll bow out.
en: I promise.  Dead Poets honor.  Come with me tonight, then if you don't want to see me again, I swear I'll bow out.

text: God, if Chet found out he'd...
en: God, if Chet found out he'd...

text: Chet won't know anything.  We'll sit in back and sneak away as soon as it's over.
en: Chet won't know anything.  We'll sit in back and sneak away as soon as it's over.

text: Knox, if you promise that this will be the end of it-
en: Knox, if you promise that this will be the end of it-

text: Dead Poets honor.
en: Dead Poets honor.

text: What is that?
en: What is that?

text: My Word
en: My Word

text: It's just for a little while. You promised.
en: It's just for a little while. You promised.

text: "We went to the woods because we wanted to suck all the marrow out of life." Anybody want to read?
en: "We went to the woods because we wanted to suck all the marrow out of life." Anybody want to read?

text: I have something.
en: I have something.

text: Where are we now, Commodus?  Can you see the camp?  My Gods!  The air is turning into ice!
en: Where are we now, Commodus?  Can you see the camp?  My Gods!  The air is turning into ice!

text: We're nearly there, Lucilla.
en: We're nearly there, Lucilla.

text: That's what you told me two days ago!
en: That's what you told me two days ago!

text: Will you please get back in your wagon?  And stay there?
en: Will you please get back in your wagon?  And stay there?

text: I'm tired of being stuck in that wagon.
en: I'm tired of being stuck in that wagon.

text: Where is my father?
en: Where is my father?

text: Where is the emperor and the army, soldier?
en: Where is the emperor and the army, soldier?

text: I see the emperor's little boy has finally caught up with the army.
en: I see the emperor's little boy has finally caught up with the army.

text: Let's hope he doesn't start giving orders.
en: Let's hope he doesn't start giving orders.

text: Welcome back from your great triumph Narcissus Meridas.  My father sends his heart felt praise.  Sadly, Marcus is in dark humors -- nothing to worry about, but he needs rest. Likely just the weather.
en: Welcome back from your great triumph Narcissus Meridas.  My father sends his heart felt praise.  Sadly, Marcus is in dark humors -- nothing to worry about, but he needs rest. Likely just the weather.

text: Respectfully Caesar, Quintus and I must report.
en: Respectfully Caesar, Quintus and I must report.

text: Of course, but not now.  However, if he continues to be unwell, you may report to me.
en: Of course, but not now.  However, if he continues to be unwell, you may report to me.

text: Gladly, Caesar.  And, if you'd like we can take you for a tour of the front at first light.
en: Gladly, Caesar.  And, if you'd like we can take you for a tour of the front at first light.

text: I'm certain father will be in better humors by then.  Now, honor us with your presence at dinner.  I'll join you as soon as I see my father's physician.
en: I'm certain father will be in better humors by then.  Now, honor us with your presence at dinner.  I'll join you as soon as I see my father's physician.

text: What the hell was all that about?
en: What the hell was all that about?

text: What the hell do you think it was about?  There's nothing an unproved heir to the throne likes less than glaring competence in others.
en: What the hell do you think it was about?  There's nothing an unproved heir to the throne likes less than glaring competence in others.

text: Why don't we try to keep politics out of the conversation.
en: Why don't we try to keep politics out of the conversation.

text: Well, we can try...
en: Well, we can try...

text: No future-telling, please, I've been terrified enough for one day.
en: No future-telling, please, I've been terrified enough for one day.

text: Narcissus!  Terrified?  You?  The only thing he's scared of is me.
en: Narcissus!  Terrified?  You?  The only thing he's scared of is me.

text: You know our two most senior Senators:  Gaius Cantus and Falco Verus?
en: You know our two most senior Senators:  Gaius Cantus and Falco Verus?

text: Only from a distance.
en: Only from a distance.

text: More wine, sister?  Surely you can drink more than that.
en: More wine, sister?  Surely you can drink more than that.

text: I was suddenly thinking about going to bed.
en: I was suddenly thinking about going to bed.

text: Oh, stay...  Don't you want to join the chorus of praises for Narcissus' glory?  Just remember, he is a married man.
en: Oh, stay...  Don't you want to join the chorus of praises for Narcissus' glory?  Just remember, he is a married man.

text: Do you expect Marcus to be well enough by morning for an audience?
en: Do you expect Marcus to be well enough by morning for an audience?

text: That's difficult to say, general.
en: That's difficult to say, general.

text: Perhaps, Master Galen, you may say.
en: Perhaps, Master Galen, you may say.

text: I would venture, with all respect: the Emperor's health is the business of every soul in the empire.
en: I would venture, with all respect: the Emperor's health is the business of every soul in the empire.

text: Yes!  The days of Imperial Prerogative and disdain for the Senate are over -- thanks to your father!  Now report to the Senate, Master Galen:  what is Marcus' state?
en: Yes!  The days of Imperial Prerogative and disdain for the Senate are over -- thanks to your father!  Now report to the Senate, Master Galen:  what is Marcus' state?

text: The battle was won, today, and I prefer to believe it was a gift of Janus, the eldest God of Rome.  God of my ancestors.
en: The battle was won, today, and I prefer to believe it was a gift of Janus, the eldest God of Rome.  God of my ancestors.

text: God of passages and changes?
en: God of passages and changes?

text: I believe we are arriving in an enlightened age; an age of peace that will bring Rome her greatest glory.  Thanks to Marcus Aurelius.
en: I believe we are arriving in an enlightened age; an age of peace that will bring Rome her greatest glory.  Thanks to Marcus Aurelius.

text: You know, general, there is a Gate of Janus in Rome which is only closed in time of peace.  Sadly, it has remained open for three hundred years.
en: You know, general, there is a Gate of Janus in Rome which is only closed in time of peace.  Sadly, it has remained open for three hundred years.

text: I've read of it.
en: I've read of it.

text: But have never been?
en: But have never been?

text: My only visits to Rome, Senator, have been through books.  But the war's over, time to close the door of war once and for all.
en: My only visits to Rome, Senator, have been through books.  But the war's over, time to close the door of war once and for all.

text: Then you'd be out of a job.
en: Then you'd be out of a job.

text: Gladly Caesar.
en: Gladly Caesar.

text: Or perhaps into a new one.  But here's to your God and the courage of our legions...
en: Or perhaps into a new one.  But here's to your God and the courage of our legions...

text: Quite so.  Narcissus and his courageous men; may they live long to serve Rome...
en: Quite so.  Narcissus and his courageous men; may they live long to serve Rome...

text: And Caesar!  Let's not forget to serve Caesar!
en: And Caesar!  Let's not forget to serve Caesar!

text: I back Rome against all her enemies -- if that answer disappoints you, I'm not a politician...
en: I back Rome against all her enemies -- if that answer disappoints you, I'm not a politician...

text: Oh, but with the army behind you, you could become extremely political.  Not a Republican by any chance?
en: Oh, but with the army behind you, you could become extremely political.  Not a Republican by any chance?

text: A republican is a man who strives to create equality among all classes.  At the core he's a man who believes in doing what's right.
en: A republican is a man who strives to create equality among all classes.  At the core he's a man who believes in doing what's right.

text: The trouble is defining exactly what 'right' is.
en: The trouble is defining exactly what 'right' is.

text: We all know what right is, Senator.
en: We all know what right is, Senator.

text: I would say there's nothing more dangerous than a man who knows what 'right' is.
en: I would say there's nothing more dangerous than a man who knows what 'right' is.

text: The dangerous man, Caesar, is the man who doesn't care.
en: The dangerous man, Caesar, is the man who doesn't care.

text: But they're not destroyed, not yet.
en: But they're not destroyed, not yet.

text: Do we really need to repair this fort?  It seems like an expensive undertaking.  I propose we burn it to the ground.  That way if the Germans cross the Danube here there will be nothing to help them build an offensive position.
en: Do we really need to repair this fort?  It seems like an expensive undertaking.  I propose we burn it to the ground.  That way if the Germans cross the Danube here there will be nothing to help them build an offensive position.

text: Commodus, it's we who are going on the offensive.
en: Commodus, it's we who are going on the offensive.

text: The fort helps position us for a final invasion in the spring when they're most vulnerable.
en: The fort helps position us for a final invasion in the spring when they're most vulnerable.

text: Commodus -- listen to Narcissus, listen to the man who has never lost a battle for Rome!  You're young with years ahead of you before you gain the experience to wear the purple!
en: Commodus -- listen to Narcissus, listen to the man who has never lost a battle for Rome!  You're young with years ahead of you before you gain the experience to wear the purple!

text: I want you to start your work for the last phase of the campaign.
en: I want you to start your work for the last phase of the campaign.

text: I will, Marcus.  But you're going to be well enough to direct it yourself.
en: I will, Marcus.  But you're going to be well enough to direct it yourself.

text: I've made so many mistakes, Narcissus.  We all put off the very last duties of our lives because we're afraid of admitting when our lives are over.
en: I've made so many mistakes, Narcissus.  We all put off the very last duties of our lives because we're afraid of admitting when our lives are over.

text: There's no reason to say that. Everyone knows you're going to be well.  I had Servis groom your horse for a triumphal visit to the front at first light.
en: There's no reason to say that. Everyone knows you're going to be well.  I had Servis groom your horse for a triumphal visit to the front at first light.

text: Servis made it through again?
en: Servis made it through again?

text: He's like you, sir, too tough for the Gods to swallow.
en: He's like you, sir, too tough for the Gods to swallow.

text: If I'd ever had a sign that you wanted to rule I would have... no, again, it's my own bullheadedness. Narcissus, I should have adopted you years ago.  And now the Gods are begging me to make you my son!
en: If I'd ever had a sign that you wanted to rule I would have... no, again, it's my own bullheadedness. Narcissus, I should have adopted you years ago.  And now the Gods are begging me to make you my son!

text: Commodus is just a young man, he'll learn what you had to learn.
en: Commodus is just a young man, he'll learn what you had to learn.

text: It's not because he's young, it's because he's ignorant and arrogant. His sister is a better man.  That's why I have undertaken to begin sweeping changes in the relationship between the emperor and the Senate.
en: It's not because he's young, it's because he's ignorant and arrogant. His sister is a better man.  That's why I have undertaken to begin sweeping changes in the relationship between the emperor and the Senate.

text: So I understand.
en: So I understand.

text: Everyone talking about it?  I wouldn't wonder.  All I seek is a genuine balance of power between the Emperor and the Senate.  Thus I have transferred legal power -- which was theirs to begin with -- back to the Senators.  This includes a shared right to taxation too but some bite in the plan.  It's a start, only a start.  If the Emperor and the Senate can share power then the people will be ready to take their share.  This means Commodus has to bend; does he strike you as that type?
en: Everyone talking about it?  I wouldn't wonder.  All I seek is a genuine balance of power between the Emperor and the Senate.  Thus I have transferred legal power -- which was theirs to begin with -- back to the Senators.  This includes a shared right to taxation too but some bite in the plan.  It's a start, only a start.  If the Emperor and the Senate can share power then the people will be ready to take their share.  This means Commodus has to bend; does he strike you as that type?

text: You're too hard on him.  He is a strong young man, with you as his guide...
en: You're too hard on him.  He is a strong young man, with you as his guide...

text: A man should be upright, not be kept upright.  History shows us that a good general is quick to recognize opportunities -- even if it means making a complete about face at the last minute.  I want you to consider becoming my heir.
en: A man should be upright, not be kept upright.  History shows us that a good general is quick to recognize opportunities -- even if it means making a complete about face at the last minute.  I want you to consider becoming my heir.

text: Marcus, you honor me, but I'm a soldier, politics scare the hell out of me.
en: Marcus, you honor me, but I'm a soldier, politics scare the hell out of me.

text: The Senators admire you.
en: The Senators admire you.

text: They fear me.
en: They fear me.

text: They fear change.  The new Caesar must be honest enough to know when the emperorship is no longer feasible.  You could be the one, the Emperor, the man who oversees the rebirth of the Republic.
en: They fear change.  The new Caesar must be honest enough to know when the emperorship is no longer feasible.  You could be the one, the Emperor, the man who oversees the rebirth of the Republic.

text: I'll do anything in my power to help you restore the Republic but I can't be that power.
en: I'll do anything in my power to help you restore the Republic but I can't be that power.

text: You and my father have become very close.  Perhaps one day I may say the same for us.
en: You and my father have become very close.  Perhaps one day I may say the same for us.

text: You flatter me, Caesar.
en: You flatter me, Caesar.

text: Being as close, I'm certain you've noticed what we all have noticed.
en: Being as close, I'm certain you've noticed what we all have noticed.

text: Caesar?
en: Caesar?

text: That this illness has clouded his mind.
en: That this illness has clouded his mind.

text: I thought all good generals were quick to recognize opportunities.
en: I thought all good generals were quick to recognize opportunities.

text: Sneaking around with your brother?
en: Sneaking around with your brother?

text: Without him.  He'd be weeping if he overheard that.  Well?  The idea of you as my adopted brother is very... exciting.
en: Without him.  He'd be weeping if he overheard that.  Well?  The idea of you as my adopted brother is very... exciting.

text: I'm not fit for the job and as a matter of fact I'm not taking the job.
en: I'm not fit for the job and as a matter of fact I'm not taking the job.

text: Why do you keep playing at being so humble?  It's a little embarrassing.
en: Why do you keep playing at being so humble?  It's a little embarrassing.

text: Why do you play at being drunk?
en: Why do you play at being drunk?

text: How do you know I am playing?  Well, the clown is always harmless.  Isn't that right?  And how did you ever get to know me so well?  The last we spent any time together I was fourteen.  I think you know me better than my father.  He's going to die, isn't he?
en: How do you know I am playing?  Well, the clown is always harmless.  Isn't that right?  And how did you ever get to know me so well?  The last we spent any time together I was fourteen.  I think you know me better than my father.  He's going to die, isn't he?

text: I don't believe that.  He's got the best doctor in the world and a will of iron.  You know we're preparing for a full-blown invasion of Germany.
en: I don't believe that.  He's got the best doctor in the world and a will of iron.  You know we're preparing for a full-blown invasion of Germany.

text: Of course I know -- who do you think is paying for it?  The Emperor himself, didn't you know?  Why do you think Commodus came rushing up to the front?  Burning patriotism? Filial love?  He wants to be sure when he takes over there's enough cash left in the treasury to... play Emperor.  Watch out for him, Narcissus; he's inexperienced, but... be careful.
en: Of course I know -- who do you think is paying for it?  The Emperor himself, didn't you know?  Why do you think Commodus came rushing up to the front?  Burning patriotism? Filial love?  He wants to be sure when he takes over there's enough cash left in the treasury to... play Emperor.  Watch out for him, Narcissus; he's inexperienced, but... be careful.

text: He's very realistic.
en: He's very realistic.

text: Isn't the helmet magnificent?
en: Isn't the helmet magnificent?

text: I made the plume from a quail feather.
en: I made the plume from a quail feather.

text: Much more colorful than the ones we wear.  And, of course, less dented.
en: Much more colorful than the ones we wear.  And, of course, less dented.

text: Well, you wanted the girls to have the best teachers.
en: Well, you wanted the girls to have the best teachers.

text: Greeks?
en: Greeks?

text: Athenians...
en: Athenians...

text: What about their philosophy lessons?
en: What about their philosophy lessons?

text: They're studying with Cynics.
en: They're studying with Cynics.

text: Of course...
en: Of course...

text: You need to come home!
en: You need to come home!

text: I can see that...
en: I can see that...

text: The battle is over.  The war is over.  You've won!
en: The battle is over.  The war is over.  You've won!

text: If you win, you know, you have to stay.  It's the losers who get to go home.  Besides, I'm not so sure it is over.  Centurions report enemy scouts probing our lines.
en: If you win, you know, you have to stay.  It's the losers who get to go home.  Besides, I'm not so sure it is over.  Centurions report enemy scouts probing our lines.

text: This is our oil from our estates?
en: This is our oil from our estates?

text: I've been overseeing production myself for the past three years, you'll be surprised at how wonderful our oil has become.
en: I've been overseeing production myself for the past three years, you'll be surprised at how wonderful our oil has become.

text: Very fancy.  Did you design the bottle?
en: Very fancy.  Did you design the bottle?

text: Who else?  I'm the one who runs the estates while you're here risking everything we have for the glory of Rome!  Or for the glory of you!
en: Who else?  I'm the one who runs the estates while you're here risking everything we have for the glory of Rome!  Or for the glory of you!

text: I'm a soldier -- we're at war.  I can't stay home tending the damned olive groves?
en: I'm a soldier -- we're at war.  I can't stay home tending the damned olive groves?

text: We don't need your help we're doing great on our own.
en: We don't need your help we're doing great on our own.

text: I want to come home, of course I do, I'd have to be mad not to want that. It's just that Marcus trusts me.
en: I want to come home, of course I do, I'd have to be mad not to want that. It's just that Marcus trusts me.

text: Let him trust Quintus.
en: Let him trust Quintus.

text: Quintus is overly idealistic.
en: Quintus is overly idealistic.

text: I never knew a more idealistic man than you.
en: I never knew a more idealistic man than you.

text: Me?  Well, I believe in Rome... you'd have to after what I've seen, how people outside the empire treat each other.
en: Me?  Well, I believe in Rome... you'd have to after what I've seen, how people outside the empire treat each other.

text: I don't even want to imagine the things you've seen...
en: I don't even want to imagine the things you've seen...

text: What you don't want to imagine is the things I've done.
en: What you don't want to imagine is the things I've done.

text: Don't stop to visit -- take the children straight home and I'll follow as soon as I can.
en: Don't stop to visit -- take the children straight home and I'll follow as soon as I can.

text: Tomorrow?
en: Tomorrow?

text: As soon as I can.
en: As soon as I can.

text: On your honor as a Roman officer, daddy?
en: On your honor as a Roman officer, daddy?

text: On my honor as your daddy...
en: On my honor as your daddy...

text: Marcus Aurelius has died.
en: Marcus Aurelius has died.

text: He left us at dawn.
en: He left us at dawn.

text: Serious stuff...
en: Serious stuff...

text: Centurions on both sides of the river are convinced the Germans will try one last offensive.  They've got nothing to lose and it's so very like them.
en: Centurions on both sides of the river are convinced the Germans will try one last offensive.  They've got nothing to lose and it's so very like them.

text: I'm ordering a general stand-down in preparation for withdrawal back across the Danube.
en: I'm ordering a general stand-down in preparation for withdrawal back across the Danube.

text: We have to stop the Germans now!
en: We have to stop the Germans now!

text: We must obey our emperor and the Senate.
en: We must obey our emperor and the Senate.

text: I met with Falco, and the Senators have agreed to call for a truce with the Germans.
en: I met with Falco, and the Senators have agreed to call for a truce with the Germans.

text: Forgive me, Caesar, but do two Senator represent the mood of the whole Senate or the will of the Roman people?  Besides, every truce we make with the Germans they break!
en: Forgive me, Caesar, but do two Senator represent the mood of the whole Senate or the will of the Roman people?  Besides, every truce we make with the Germans they break!

text: They won't break this one.
en: They won't break this one.

text: Apparently my opinion wasn't needed.
en: Apparently my opinion wasn't needed.

text: Everyone knew you would have been outspoken against this deal.
en: Everyone knew you would have been outspoken against this deal.

text: What deal?
en: What deal?

text: Rome is going to pay an allotment to the German tribes on an annual basis.
en: Rome is going to pay an allotment to the German tribes on an annual basis.

text: Rome is going to pay tribute -- like a defeated nation begging for mercy? Have you told your troops that?
en: Rome is going to pay tribute -- like a defeated nation begging for mercy? Have you told your troops that?

text: My troops don't make policy.
en: My troops don't make policy.

text: Well, they die for it!
en: Well, they die for it!

text: On his death bed I promised Marcus I would complete our work here.  The Senate may be vacillating, but I have the army behind me.  I'm taking half a cohort and restocking that fort.
en: On his death bed I promised Marcus I would complete our work here.  The Senate may be vacillating, but I have the army behind me.  I'm taking half a cohort and restocking that fort.

text: I can't let you do that.
en: I can't let you do that.

text: Where is my family?
en: Where is my family?

text: Cooperate and they will be returned to your estate.  I could have executed you.
en: Cooperate and they will be returned to your estate.  I could have executed you.

text: And my army would have thrown your body into the Danube.
en: And my army would have thrown your body into the Danube.

text: The army is a problem.  They love you.  You have led them from victory to victory in the name of Rome and they love you.  And after all, you're just a hothead acting from a misguided sense of loyalty -- who could fault you for that?  Thus have I reached a compromise with the Senate over your fate: instead of executing you, I'm sending you to Rome where you will be tried...
en: The army is a problem.  They love you.  You have led them from victory to victory in the name of Rome and they love you.  And after all, you're just a hothead acting from a misguided sense of loyalty -- who could fault you for that?  Thus have I reached a compromise with the Senate over your fate: instead of executing you, I'm sending you to Rome where you will be tried...

text: On what charge?
en: On what charge?

text: Insubordination.  To the Emperor... and the Senate.
en: Insubordination.  To the Emperor... and the Senate.

text: Quintus will tell the army that you are being called to Rome to celebrate your victory.  They will hear that you are living in luxury. He will let them feel you have betrayed them for the good life. And soon the army won't even remember your name.
en: Quintus will tell the army that you are being called to Rome to celebrate your victory.  They will hear that you are living in luxury. He will let them feel you have betrayed them for the good life. And soon the army won't even remember your name.

text: The Senate too?
en: The Senate too?

text: The moment you returned from the battle your options were clear.  If you are a friend to neither side, legate, you must be an enemy to both.  We needed to know what you believed.
en: The moment you returned from the battle your options were clear.  If you are a friend to neither side, legate, you must be an enemy to both.  We needed to know what you believed.

text: I hope you live to see what I believe...
en: I hope you live to see what I believe...

text: You on your way to trial, too, general?  Or do you think they've already had our trial?
en: You on your way to trial, too, general?  Or do you think they've already had our trial?

text: Why you?
en: Why you?

text: My loyalties... were in doubt.
en: My loyalties... were in doubt.

text: Fools to let us both live; we'll be our own best witnesses at our trial.
en: Fools to let us both live; we'll be our own best witnesses at our trial.

text: That's what worries me...
en: That's what worries me...

text: The Senate is out to sink you.  I swear it, Caesar, your generosity is being repaid with public attacks on your honor.  Your enemies want you weak enough so by the first of Janus when you must be confirmed the Senate will be able to deny you.
en: The Senate is out to sink you.  I swear it, Caesar, your generosity is being repaid with public attacks on your honor.  Your enemies want you weak enough so by the first of Janus when you must be confirmed the Senate will be able to deny you.

text: How can they?  Who else is there?  I have no heir...
en: How can they?  Who else is there?  I have no heir...

text: Is this Rome?  Are we just going to be executed?
en: Is this Rome?  Are we just going to be executed?

text: They can't... if this is Rome...
en: They can't... if this is Rome...

text: You're a legate in the Roman army. Huh...?  and you act like one. What was your crime?
en: You're a legate in the Roman army. Huh...?  and you act like one. What was your crime?

text: I killed too many barbarians.
en: I killed too many barbarians.

text: I'm a Greek, thank you.  And I was brought up believing Romans were the barbarians.  Give our new colleague some of the Cretan white.  Relax, tell me everything, I'm your friend.
en: I'm a Greek, thank you.  And I was brought up believing Romans were the barbarians.  Give our new colleague some of the Cretan white.  Relax, tell me everything, I'm your friend.

text: Who the hell are you?
en: Who the hell are you?

text: I am the man who might save your life -- give you a bit more life at any rate.  I am Proximo Palindromos head of this gladiatorial school which is named after me.  I own this school and everything that's in it.  You're in it!  But why?  What did a Roman general do to get himself condemned to the Colosseum? Understand, we usually get corn thieves and pick pockets.  Please, I separated you from the others because...  ... my nose tells me you've been condemned for important reasons.
en: I am the man who might save your life -- give you a bit more life at any rate.  I am Proximo Palindromos head of this gladiatorial school which is named after me.  I own this school and everything that's in it.  You're in it!  But why?  What did a Roman general do to get himself condemned to the Colosseum? Understand, we usually get corn thieves and pick pockets.  Please, I separated you from the others because...  ... my nose tells me you've been condemned for important reasons.

text: Condemned?  Aren't I owed a trial before being condemned?
en: Condemned?  Aren't I owed a trial before being condemned?

text: General, all I know is you have been condemned to the Colosseum, and a trial is nowhere to be seen.
en: General, all I know is you have been condemned to the Colosseum, and a trial is nowhere to be seen.

text: Impossible!  Every citizen has a right to trial -- this is Rome!
en: Impossible!  Every citizen has a right to trial -- this is Rome!

text: Thank you.  What is going to happen tomorrow?  Exactly?
en: Thank you.  What is going to happen tomorrow?  Exactly?

text: You are to be killed, exactly. They'll give you a sporting chance, but just enough to make your murder... entertaining.  Romans like to mix their metaphors:  laughter with their executions, you know?  If you survive, though, you will become a gladiator.  A gladiator at least gets a fair fight.
en: You are to be killed, exactly. They'll give you a sporting chance, but just enough to make your murder... entertaining.  Romans like to mix their metaphors:  laughter with their executions, you know?  If you survive, though, you will become a gladiator.  A gladiator at least gets a fair fight.

text: Death is a very light thing for you.
en: Death is a very light thing for you.

text: Death is... everything for me.  Now you have to go to your cell, and I to dicker with Jerses... you'll be fed well.  I want you to be fit as you can be; I want you to win for me tomorrow!  I want all my gladiators to win and be happy!  Besides, I've never owned a Roman general before.
en: Death is... everything for me.  Now you have to go to your cell, and I to dicker with Jerses... you'll be fed well.  I want you to be fit as you can be; I want you to win for me tomorrow!  I want all my gladiators to win and be happy!  Besides, I've never owned a Roman general before.

text: Take your hands off me animal!
en: Take your hands off me animal!

text: Chain him.
en: Chain him.

text: Sorry but I have to get at least one fight out of you otherwise I won't even get back the cost of the bribe I had to pay the arena slaves to get you here.  I know what you're trying to do:  kill yourself and trust in the Roman tradition of justice that the emperor will let your family survive and keep their lands.  The only thing you have accomplishes is to prove you're a very important individual.  You make me feel good about my investment!  And that puking pig Jerses -- he won't even discuss you.  Both of you have clamped mouths!  But I love all my fighters -- I'll find out about your family.  And about you.  That I promise.
en: Sorry but I have to get at least one fight out of you otherwise I won't even get back the cost of the bribe I had to pay the arena slaves to get you here.  I know what you're trying to do:  kill yourself and trust in the Roman tradition of justice that the emperor will let your family survive and keep their lands.  The only thing you have accomplishes is to prove you're a very important individual.  You make me feel good about my investment!  And that puking pig Jerses -- he won't even discuss you.  Both of you have clamped mouths!  But I love all my fighters -- I'll find out about your family.  And about you.  That I promise.

text: I refuse to be your slave.  I refuse --
en: I refuse to be your slave.  I refuse --

text: -- to fight?  We'll see...
en: -- to fight?  We'll see...

text: Don't you think you should at least wave?
en: Don't you think you should at least wave?

text: Why?  Then they'll notice when I'm gone.  Well.  I'm making a public appearance aren't I?
en: Why?  Then they'll notice when I'm gone.  Well.  I'm making a public appearance aren't I?

text: Caesar, ignore them.
en: Caesar, ignore them.

text: Ignore that?!  The sooner we leave this disgusting place the better.
en: Ignore that?!  The sooner we leave this disgusting place the better.

text: At least stay for the running of the animals.  You are paying for it you know...
en: At least stay for the running of the animals.  You are paying for it you know...

text: Gods of hell!  This must cost a fortune!  How many days is this going to go on?
en: Gods of hell!  This must cost a fortune!  How many days is this going to go on?

text: Until your confirmation date.
en: Until your confirmation date.

text: Legate Narcissus Meridas, general of the Spanish Felix Legions!  I'm proud to have you in my school!  Now, show them what you can do!
en: Legate Narcissus Meridas, general of the Spanish Felix Legions!  I'm proud to have you in my school!  Now, show them what you can do!

text: I'm not a gladiator.  I refuse to fight.
en: I'm not a gladiator.  I refuse to fight.

text: Then, you'll die...  Just, know this:  because you asked I asked:  I'm sorry but...
en: Then, you'll die...  Just, know this:  because you asked I asked:  I'm sorry but...

text: They hate me, they really hate me don't they?
en: They hate me, they really hate me don't they?

text: Maybe you should get married.  Pick one of your cousins, it would demonstrate a profound stability.
en: Maybe you should get married.  Pick one of your cousins, it would demonstrate a profound stability.

text: I should at least have you, don't you think?
en: I should at least have you, don't you think?

text: If you get me pregnant with a boy he'll be a double direct heir and will end up killing you for the throne.
en: If you get me pregnant with a boy he'll be a double direct heir and will end up killing you for the throne.

text: Did Narcissus die today?  Wasn't this his day to die?
en: Did Narcissus die today?  Wasn't this his day to die?

text: I'm sure, I don't know.
en: I'm sure, I don't know.

text: Tribuus, what happened in the arena? Was Narcissus killed?
en: Tribuus, what happened in the arena? Was Narcissus killed?

text: He must have been.  He was on the list of prisoners to be executed.
en: He must have been.  He was on the list of prisoners to be executed.

text: No one in Rome has ever heard of him.  Do you want to remind those few in the Senate who have?  The whole sordid thing is far beneath your position to begin with.  Forget about him.
en: No one in Rome has ever heard of him.  Do you want to remind those few in the Senate who have?  The whole sordid thing is far beneath your position to begin with.  Forget about him.

text: Let some time pass... then ask, quietly, without anyone knowing it comes from me.
en: Let some time pass... then ask, quietly, without anyone knowing it comes from me.

text: General, do you realize what happened out there today?
en: General, do you realize what happened out there today?

text: I didn't get killed and everyone else did.
en: I didn't get killed and everyone else did.

text: That's one way to look at it.
en: That's one way to look at it.

text: You won an impossible fight.  You got the attention of the crowd, legate...
en: You won an impossible fight.  You got the attention of the crowd, legate...

text: This is Cos, this precocious young man.  A scribe for the Daily Action.  I've invited him to write a small piece about you...
en: This is Cos, this precocious young man.  A scribe for the Daily Action.  I've invited him to write a small piece about you...

text: I'm mentioning you in tomorrow's athlete's section, legate.  So, I'd love to know your birth sign; it effects how people bet.  And perhaps you could tell me a bit about... well, who are you?
en: I'm mentioning you in tomorrow's athlete's section, legate.  So, I'd love to know your birth sign; it effects how people bet.  And perhaps you could tell me a bit about... well, who are you?

text: And like children everywhere they scream "freedom" the most when they desire it least.  I beg you, please continue, Caesar.
en: And like children everywhere they scream "freedom" the most when they desire it least.  I beg you, please continue, Caesar.

text: At the opening of the month of Janus, I will ask this noble body to confirm my emperorship...
en: At the opening of the month of Janus, I will ask this noble body to confirm my emperorship...

text: Those are priceless sculptures.
en: Those are priceless sculptures.

text: I want every single thing that belonged to my father out of this house!  If it's worth something then sell it!
en: I want every single thing that belonged to my father out of this house!  If it's worth something then sell it!

text: It's disgusting!  Animals!  I had to come here under armed guard.  Slaves -- get this junk out of my sight.
en: It's disgusting!  Animals!  I had to come here under armed guard.  Slaves -- get this junk out of my sight.

text: Commodus is heaving out every thing that belonged to our father.  Except that he can't heave out his ghost.
en: Commodus is heaving out every thing that belonged to our father.  Except that he can't heave out his ghost.

text: Throw it down into the streets! Down into the Forum.  If it's my father they want then give him to them!
en: Throw it down into the streets! Down into the Forum.  If it's my father they want then give him to them!

text: Yes.  You know, that's not a bad idea.
en: Yes.  You know, that's not a bad idea.

text: Maybe it'll crush Gaius.
en: Maybe it'll crush Gaius.

text: I'm serious.
en: I'm serious.

text: So am I.
en: So am I.

text: We can crush Gaius another way. What if you do throw something to the people they really want?  Make them a gift of food.
en: We can crush Gaius another way. What if you do throw something to the people they really want?  Make them a gift of food.

text: Give away food?
en: Give away food?

text: Sacks of grain, even bread.  I own the grain licenses for the military, I can arrange to divert a shipment bound for the army of the Danube.
en: Sacks of grain, even bread.  I own the grain licenses for the military, I can arrange to divert a shipment bound for the army of the Danube.

text: Take grain away from the army?
en: Take grain away from the army?

text: Make a gift to the people.  It's your money anyway so it's only fair.
en: Make a gift to the people.  It's your money anyway so it's only fair.

text: How about in the forum.  Right in front of the Senate.
en: How about in the forum.  Right in front of the Senate.

text: If I may be so bold...
en: If I may be so bold...

text: For the gods, spit it out!
en: For the gods, spit it out!

text: Why not do it in the Colosseum?
en: Why not do it in the Colosseum?

text: Caesar, let me sponsor your first wager in the arena.
en: Caesar, let me sponsor your first wager in the arena.

text: I wouldn't know who to bet on.
en: I wouldn't know who to bet on.

text: Why isn't he dead?  Damn you, you promised me he would be dead!
en: Why isn't he dead?  Damn you, you promised me he would be dead!

text: Caesar, I did my best.  The Colosseum isn't under my control...
en: Caesar, I did my best.  The Colosseum isn't under my control...

text: Kill him!  Tribuus -- execute that bastard.
en: Kill him!  Tribuus -- execute that bastard.

text: You can't do that!  You came here to turn the crowd around not make them hate you.
en: You can't do that!  You came here to turn the crowd around not make them hate you.

text: Tribuus... tomorrow.
en: Tribuus... tomorrow.

text: You're coming back?
en: You're coming back?

text: I have to come back.  If I don't come back the people will think I'm a coward.  Tribuus, tomorrow he dies -- I want his blood on the sand.  Do you understand?
en: I have to come back.  If I don't come back the people will think I'm a coward.  Tribuus, tomorrow he dies -- I want his blood on the sand.  Do you understand?

text: Yes, Caesar?
en: Yes, Caesar?

text: Jerses -- tomorrow...
en: Jerses -- tomorrow...

text: At the end of the day I was approached by the Golden Pompeii Olive Oil company.  Small, but profitable.  They asked if you would endorse their oil.  We could get some very nice posters.  Make some very big money...
en: At the end of the day I was approached by the Golden Pompeii Olive Oil company.  Small, but profitable.  They asked if you would endorse their oil.  We could get some very nice posters.  Make some very big money...

text: What would the poster say: "Narcissus would kill for a taste of Golden Pompeii Olive Oil?"
en: What would the poster say: "Narcissus would kill for a taste of Golden Pompeii Olive Oil?"

text: Think about it!  Just think about it!
en: Think about it!  Just think about it!

text: Who are you?  Narcissus The Good?  I have heard of Narcissus Meridas. That's who I hear you are.
en: Who are you?  Narcissus The Good?  I have heard of Narcissus Meridas. That's who I hear you are.

text: You're hearing about somebody else.
en: You're hearing about somebody else.

text: How did you get condemned to the arena without a trial?
en: How did you get condemned to the arena without a trial?

text: When the Senate and the Emperor agree miracles can happen.
en: When the Senate and the Emperor agree miracles can happen.

text: Would you support the Senate if they would give you a trial?  You'd have to give me your word.
en: Would you support the Senate if they would give you a trial?  You'd have to give me your word.

text: I need to give you my word when yours is worth nothing?
en: I need to give you my word when yours is worth nothing?

text: You're a citizen and a soldier.  Not a gladiator.
en: You're a citizen and a soldier.  Not a gladiator.

text: You don't know how wrong you are.
en: You don't know how wrong you are.

text: General, hang on... drink slowly. You are blessed by the Gods to have a physician and a Divine of Janus with you tonight.  A fan sent them to you.
en: General, hang on... drink slowly. You are blessed by the Gods to have a physician and a Divine of Janus with you tonight.  A fan sent them to you.

text: Alive... I'm alive...
en: Alive... I'm alive...

text: It's my brother's neck you want, not mine.
en: It's my brother's neck you want, not mine.

text: Yours will do!
en: Yours will do!

text: Proximo, if this is her doctor he's an assassin.
en: Proximo, if this is her doctor he's an assassin.

text: Don't be ridiculous.
en: Don't be ridiculous.

text: I came here to see that you stay alive.  The people need a living breathing alternative to Commodus, a hero.
en: I came here to see that you stay alive.  The people need a living breathing alternative to Commodus, a hero.

text: You mean a symbol of someone who doesn't exist.
en: You mean a symbol of someone who doesn't exist.

text: But you do exist.  Narcissus:  hero of the battle of the Danube.
en: But you do exist.  Narcissus:  hero of the battle of the Danube.

text: Twenty years I've led men to die. For me it was the glory of Rome. But that was something.  If it wasn't that, then it was the pay or the loot of the next whore -- but that was something!  These men here are butchered for laughs!  Their lives are like jokes delivered in the back alley theaters where their death is a punch line!
en: Twenty years I've led men to die. For me it was the glory of Rome. But that was something.  If it wasn't that, then it was the pay or the loot of the next whore -- but that was something!  These men here are butchered for laughs!  Their lives are like jokes delivered in the back alley theaters where their death is a punch line!

text: For the sake of the Gods, you're not leading these men?  How like my father you are.  You 'believe'... I guess that's why you're still alive.
en: For the sake of the Gods, you're not leading these men?  How like my father you are.  You 'believe'... I guess that's why you're still alive.

text: To think I brought my daughters up on all things Roman.  Read to sleep on Catullus, Lucretius... Virgil... every night.  My beautiful daughters.
en: To think I brought my daughters up on all things Roman.  Read to sleep on Catullus, Lucretius... Virgil... every night.  My beautiful daughters.

text: Do you remember your Epictetus, that little homily we recited when we were children?  The one that was supposed to remind us we were Romans?
